pages.title: Welcome to Phishing & Email Safety
pages.subtitle: Learn how phishing works and how to spot it before damage happens.

pages.title: What is phishing?
pages.text:
Phishing is a social engineering attack where someone pretends to be a trusted source to trick you into clicking a link, opening an attachment, or sharing sensitive information.

Social engineering means manipulating people into revealing confidential information or performing unsafe actions.

These messages are designed to look legitimate and may target people through email, text messages, fake websites, or even phone calls.

The goal is often to steal passwords, deliver malware, or gain access to internal systems.

image.alt: Example of a phishing email (replace later)

pages.title: Types of phishing
pages.text:
Phishing can happen through multiple communication channels.

Common types include:

  • Email phishing – Fake emails pretending to be from companies, coworkers, or vendors.
  • Smishing – Phishing messages sent through SMS or text messages.
  • Vishing – Phone calls where attackers impersonate IT staff, banks, or government agencies.

Even if the message seems legitimate, always verify before responding or clicking anything.

This example shows a smishing message impersonating the IRS.

image.alt: Examples of email, SMS, and phone phishing

pages.title: Did you know?
pages.text: Phishing messages can be highly convincing, and attackers often make small changes that are easy to miss.

bullets.title: Phishing messages can look almost identical to real ones
bullets.text: Attackers often copy logos, tone, formatting, and even email signatures to appear legitimate.

bullets.title: Some phishing attacks use look-alike domains
bullets.text: For example, micr0soft.com instead of microsoft.com — a small difference that can be easy to miss.

bullets.title: Business Email Compromise (BEC) is common
bullets.text: Attackers may impersonate a boss, executive, or vendor to request money, gift cards, wire transfers, or sensitive data.

bullets.title: Urgency is a major red flag
bullets.text: Messages like "act now," "final notice," or "your account will be locked" are designed to pressure people into mistakes.

pages.title: Phishing activity over time
pages.text:
This chart is meant to show that phishing has remained a major threat over time, with large waves of activity and continued criminal interest in phishing campaigns.

Reference: Statista – Number of phishing attacks worldwide

image.alt: Chart showing phishing attack activity over time

pages.title: Common phishing red flags
pages.text:
Most phishing attempts include warning signs if you know what to look for.

Be cautious of:

  • Urgent or threatening language.
  • Requests for passwords or MFA codes.
  • Unexpected attachments, links, or QR codes.
  • Sender addresses that do not quite match.
  • Messages that pressure you to act quickly.

When in doubt, pause and verify before taking action.

video.caption: Breakdown of a realistic phishing attempt (replace later)

pages.title: What to do if you spot phishing
pages.text:
If you believe a message is phishing:

  1. Do not click links or open attachments.
  2. Do not reply to the message.
  3. Use your email provider's report spam or report phishing feature if one is available.
  4. Report the message using your organization's approved reporting process.
  5. Delete the message after reporting, if instructed by your organization.

Reporting helps protect not just you, but everyone in your organization.
